sentences.text: Right.
So although Merke was so-- as I said before, was full of refugees, Russian, and mostly Russian that were runaway.
We had-- I remember when it was the siege of Leningrad.
And so a lot of people came there completely run down.
So it was sores over their bodies.
You know, we-- so these years in Merke, I was really growing up.
Because from, let's say, from a 12-year-old girl to a 17-year-old girl.
It's a-- it's a big difference in ages.
So-- and-- and the way I was more or less, if I think now, I think was love, in spite of all the problems that one had.
And a lot of people died.
A lot of my parents' close friends died from typhoid, from all kinds of sicknesses and from undernourishment.
But then the good thing about it was that we didn't feel-- the way-- when we were in [NON-ENGLISH], we felt really like outcasts.
Here we felt like all the Russians had a bad time.
It was a war going on.
We heard the Russian army sort of fighting on Stalingrad.
Like every day, we would have news the Russians were fighting, the Russians gave this, and the Russians-- that we felt a part of the-- sort of what was happening in Russia.
For me especially, it was quite an achievement.
Because as I said, when we came to Merke, my Russian was very poor.
When I finished, I later on finished in Dzambul, but I came top of the school.
It was a big school.
And I came out as [NON-ENGLISH] of the school.
That was a very big achievement for me personal.
I don't think that my-- my father worried about it very much, or was happy with it.
About for me, it was a great achievement, and it helped me a lot later on in life.
There were.
There were.
Yes, there were.
But-- but the Russian Jews, as we know it now, were better assimilated.
But they were-- they had a big sympathy for us, and there was a sense of Jewishness.
That's right.
It was a very good-- there were no synagogues.
I don't think that I remember anybody at that stage going to synagogue.
I don't even remember seeing a synagogue.
But-- but as I said, Merke wasn't an old beautiful city like later on when we went to Dzambul, Merke was a city, but a new on and so-- and was-- I'll tell you another story.
I don't remember even having toilets.
I remember the doors half built or houses in the wintertime we used to go into this house and we urinated there.
The sanitation was terrible.
This is why there were so many sicknesses around.
There were no synagogues.
And I think that people would be scared to go to a synagogue, even if there would be one.
I remember in school those were the times of Stalin.
And we were-- so it was everything was around Stalin, and Stalin was our hero.
I even wanted to join, at a certain stage, I wanted to join the Pioneers, which was the youth of the Communist party.
And my father said absolutely not.
Because if I would join, that might not be-- so-- that might keep up back in Russia.
And he was dreaming that the time will come that we'll go back to Poland.
I wanted to join, because one likes to belong.
One wants to be a part.
Everybody in school was a Pioneer or a Komsomol.
And I also-- I wanted to be like everybody else.
But later on I realized that that would have been very stupid, if I would do it.
But I didn't.
I never did it.
And also because I was a very bright student, they wanted me to join.
You know, they wanted me to participate in everything.
And it was a bit of a handicap.
But I never joined.
Especially a bit later on, later on, like, we had a teacher that came back.
In Russia it was a big thing if you came back from the front, let's say, many people were-- lost their legs, hands, whatever.
And I remember a teacher.
Every girl was in love with him.
He came back from the front.
He had something which was called contusio, like he was paralyzed.
One hand was paralyzed.
And he was a Cossack, and everybody which really he was not Cossack, he was a Tatar.
And everybody was-- the girls were in love with him.
We were already 16.
And he said something, he said a joke about Stalin, and a few days later he disappeared.
You know, this was the time when they were disappearing, and one was scared to open their mouth.
And we heard all the stories that this was [NON-ENGLISH], and that one that.
So-- so, we really were scared.
And my parents kept away from everything that could be-- their religion.
But everybody knew.
All the-- all their other friends were Jewish.
And although I had not Jewish friends also, but the bulk of it were a Jewish kids, Russian Jewish kids in the school even.
I remember also there was a lot going on, in-- in-- in that Merke.
Because we saw-- it's interesting what I saw on television now, the story was Chechens.
I suddenly remember it must have been 1943.
A few thousand Chechens were brought to Merke in exactly the same trains that we were going.
And they were left to die.
After a few months, there was not a single Chechen left.
I don't know what happened to them.
They got-- either they were shot or they died from that.
But they disappeared, and we were asking what happened to the Chechens, among ourselves.
And they all said, oh, they were cooperating with the Germans.
So at that stage, I was thinking that all the Chechens cooperated with the Germans.
Later on we heard different.
But at this time, so we didn't go into any politics.
We were happy to be alive, and happy so to struggle, and to survive.
Also as I said before, it's very-- it was very close to the Chinese border.
And we had bandits.
There was a no man's land.
You know, there were very tall mountains.
And we were-- Merke was very close to the mountains.
So sometimes in the morning we would see one of the people from [NON-ENGLISH] there would be killed by the bandits.
It was a lot of bandits living in the mountains.
Those were sort of like people that ran away from the Russian regime, or all kinds of people that were unhappy with what was happening in Russia.
And so we were scared of the bandits also.
Like we wouldn't venture out further.
Anyway, so among-- even now I have photographs of the children in school.
I had quite a happy time.
Mind you, the conditions in which we were going to school were terrible.
But you couldn't compare them.
They were still better than the one that I had in-- in Ural-- in Siberia.
And one day my father was working in a place which was called the artel.
This was a cooperative, where again, he was the director of the shoe department, because he knew about shoes.
And there were-- yeah, oh I almost forgot.
How did we survive?
From what my father was paid, you probably couldn't live two days.
You know, we were a family of five.
And my father was the only one working.
Because the three of us went to school.
So how did we survive?
My father and I used to say always would bring home, actually he would steal a pair of shoes or boots mostly.
And I will go-- I was the business lady in the family.
And I would go to a market, which would be, let's say, 20 kilometers away.
You couldn't sell it in the same little city.
Because everybody knew you.
So I would go away to a marketplace somewhere 20 kilometers away, and I would walk.
And I would tell them that my father was taken to the Red Army.
You know, you would always imagine things.
And I would sell.
And that would last us for a good few weeks.
And this is how we survived.
That was one of the things.
Another thing is, mostly the extras that I could sell were making life sort of possible for us to survive.
Really, this is why we were better off.
Later on, later on I would-- my mother had a dressmaker.
So out of the sheets, we would color the sheets, let's say in red, and the Cossack woman like very bright colored dresses, like the, let's say, Indian red, green, pink.
So we would die them in the color, and I would go again to the same market or to another market.
I would sell a dress, and again, we would live a few weeks from that dress.
And this is how so things-- this is how we've struggled.
And life was quite reasonable.
Until one day my father didn't come back from work.
And we were terrible upset.
We didn't know what happened he didn't come back the whole night.
The next day he came back, and he was talking something very quietly to-- to my mother.
We didn't know-- the kids didn't know what it is.
And a few days later in the middle of the night, he borrowed, or paid or bought-- I don't know what-- a horse and carriage.
And we packed whatever we had and we left-- and we left to a city which was a few 100 kilometers.
I don't know exactly how far from Merke, but it was already a large city which was called Dzambul.
With-- by horse and carriage.
And I didn't know.
Nobody knew why he did it.
Later on, you know, you didn't in Russia, you had to keep very quiet.
And you didn't even tell your own kids what was happening.
But later on I was able to find out that what happened was that he was taken to KGB.
And they told him that he has got to cooperate.
He has to work like he has to be an informer.
And my father was very unhappy with it.
But they said, if you won't do it, then you're going to jail, back to Siberia where you came from.
So he signed-- but he said that he could never do it.
So he immediately came home and planned to run away from there.
And the he was successful.
The only thing is that when you think now how many times we were uprooted, I had friends-- very close friends.
Everything was left.
And we went to that Dzambul, where that was almost I think January 1945.
That's right.
In Merke we were from 1941 to 1945.
And this was 1945 early.
I don't remember exactly the date.
But it was early 1945.
And we came to Dzambul.
And that-- a few days later because we were traveling, and we stayed on there till the end of the war.
That's exactly-- we did not absolutely-- there was no radio.
We were very isolated.
This is the whole thing that when we came back to Poland, later on I'll tell you.
It was such an unbelievable thing.
It was such a terrible shock.
What we knew in Merke, you know, you have to know what was happening there.
There was one radio, and that was KGB.
And from there, they had like loudspeakers.
So everybody that wanted a loudspeaker like that could have it.
And we listened to the radio.
But it was always on the station that they wanted it on.
So what you heard which-- which-- if the Russian-- even when the Russian army was losing, you didn't know much.
But when they-- later on you only found out that how close to Moscow the Germans were.
But that was only later.

Because when-- when they started to tell you that the Russian army won that city or won this city, I remember that Stalingrad, when they were fighting under Stalingrad, we suddenly realized that Russia must have been under Stalingrad.
Otherwise they wouldn't fight back.
But still when the war was over, we heard that.
And we were, of course, thrilled.
And with all those things we were already sort of like almost grown up, the three of us.
My sister was still young.
And my father decided to do everything possible, because Dzambul was already different to Merke.
Dzambul was a very beautiful city.
But we were-- we were so many times uprooted.
That one didn't feel-- we didn't want new friendships.
We made new friends.
There were Polish-- there was a lot of Polish refugees.
I didn't-- Dzambul already, but I didn't go to school there.
So I missed my friends from Merke.
And all our aim was now how to get out of Dzambul My father was still scared that the KGB is after him.
Look, in all these years, I've forgotten also to say that what we were always terrible scared that they would take him.
There were quite a few armies.
There was the Russian army.
There were two Polish armies.
And if my father-- this was sort of like the nightmare of our life.
Because if my father will be taken to the army, we wouldn't survive the war.
What happened, my mother was a very-- a person that was sickly, and she could never look after herself, and let alone look after a family.
So we were very scared that-- that-- but somehow he managed to stay out.
Sometimes he would pay for it.
And sometimes he managed.
Because he knew that that would be the end of all of us if he goes to the army.
So 1945 in Dzambul, he's-- all his mind concentration was how to get out.
Because he was concerned that KGB is after him, after what he did in-- in Merke.
And he tried to buy a permit.
The war was finishing, and he tried to buy a permit for money to go, like and to clean up Poland.
Because Poland was in ruins.
And he was successful.
He got some papers.
You know, in Russia you could buy everything.
He bought some papers.
And we sort of left again in that kind of cattle trains from Dzambul destination was Sosnowiec.
But in the meantime, so we were very happy that we could afford it, and we could do it.
But still then we realized how-- what was-- what happened in Europe, what happened in Poland.
As we were going, as we were coming, especially when we came to-- to Lvov, I remember that like now.
When we came to Lvov, my mother's Polish was good.
And we went out already.
We were going to buy something at the market.
And one woman said, gosh, look at that.
Hitler was killing them, and they still survived.
You know, that was for me something unbelievable.
That was the first comment.
I mean, look, I have seen anti-Semitism in Russia.
But it was-- I have seen anti-Semitism in Russia, from the Russians.
Let's say, when the Russian soldiers would come back from front, they would stand in a queue, let's say, for bread or whatever.
My father would never dare to go, to stand in a queue, it was me or my brother.
Because the soldiers would kill him.
Because they would say that-- that look at that.
We were fighting and a Jew is here.
Which wasn't true at all, because thousands and thousands of Jews, the Russian army was full of them.
But you know everybody sees what he wants to see.
But this was somehow different to what we've encountered.
You know, we-- we were not prepared to hear something like that.
That was our first encounter.
There were quite a few later on.
But it was the first thing that we heard that Hitler was killing them, and they still are around.
So this was one of-- one of the very unpleasant things that we saw straight away.
And then we saw that we just couldn't believe that there's no Jews.
Really Poland was completely what Hitler intended to do, Juden-free.
There were no Jews.
And when we came-- from that where we came from Lvov to Sosnowiec there was completely nothing.
There was no family.
You know, I was a young girl.
And I wasn't so concerned about it.
But my mother was crying day and night.
And my-- my father I've never seen my father so upset.
Because he expected his family.
He expected people.
He expected-- so that these years in Russia that now we'll come like victors.
But there was completely nobody, not one single person left of the whole family.
What he found out later on that his parents and two brothers went to Russia.
But his sister-- this is a long story.
He had a sister, a young, the youngest one, which was a very educated girl.
And he was-- she was a woman's libber at that stage already.
And she was a Communist.
She was sort of like the Secretary of the Communist Party for that whole region.
And when we came at the time in 1939, she was in prison.
So when we came to Lodz, we were told that she's in prison, and my father said, perhaps we write a letter to Stalin.
You know, we were believing that Stalin is God.
And my father helped me to write a letter.
And we did write a letter to Stalin, and we got an answer which reads like a fairy story.
But it's true.
We got-- and I'm sure Stalin didn't write.
But it was written from Stalin.
And it was signed.
And they said they'll do everything possible to find her, and to free her.
And we had information later on that she was seen on the street in Siedlce where she was imprisoned later on.
But she was shot by the Germans.
She was shot in the street by the Germans.
Somebody said, you're a Communist.
And they shot her.
No.
This is not Sosnowiec.
This is Losice.
They were all taken to Treblinka.
I'm reading now a book that came out from Poland.
And-- and so this is a new book that just came out.
It's a Holocaust, the children from Holocaust survivors.
And somebody writes about Losice.
Somebody writes, the ones that either they were taken to Treblinka or they were shot on the way.
She says that the elderly, which is very possible that my grandparents were in it, were shot on the way from Losice to Treblinka that when she was traveling the road was full of dead old people.
So this is what he found out.
That they went to Treblinka.
Where my mother lost again everybody.
She heard later on that her brother, the one that-- David, the one that was working for my father, that he even was in the uprising that he tried to run away or tried to fight with the Germans.
But-- but he died.
His wife died, and his child died.
You know, so it was terrible sad.
It was a very sad return really for my parents.
It wasn't the triumph that my father thought that-- that will happen.
And it was very sad.
And our-- so beginnings, which was 1945 I remember so really very sad.
Although I tried to make the best for-- of it.
But we-- we didn't go to Sosnowiec, but we went to Katowice, which is only a few kilometers away.
Because Katowice was a larger city.
And the city was completely-- there were no Jews.
And we got a house, a flat actual that a German vacated.
And immediately, immediately my father got in contact with New York.
And he started a committee, which was called the Jewish Committee, to help the people.
Because a lot of what was happening, we came to Sosnowiec-- to Katowice, it would be the end of September 1945.
And a lot of young people were coming from the concentration camps back to Poland in search of their families.
Also and that I remember already well at the time, quite a few people that were in hiding like, these people that were in hiding.
Like one of my mother's friends was in hiding from Losice.
And she was telling my mother what happened to the family, what happened to the ghetto, and Siedlce.
But she survived the war and went to Israel later on.
But-- but my parents were so terrible upset with everything that happened.
And that they tried to at least to help the young people.
And I remember a lot, a lot of young people coming back from Germany in search of their parents, family.
And of course not many, there were isolated cases where they would find somebody.
But on the whole, there wasn't many that did find their family.
So what the Jewish Committee did, which was a very positive thing for that time, that it would find accommodation for them.
It would feed them.
And it would also hand out clothes, also for the people that were on Aryan papers.
I-- I know a few people that-- I was helping, this was so-- I wanted to help my father in the job.
And I was feeling that this is a very important part of it.
So I was working from 1945-1946, I was working with the Jewish Committee.
I wasn't a trained social worker, but I still did the job.
And I later on, I have few people in Melbourne that remember me from that time that we were-- that I was working there.
This was sort of like a time that-- that you really think a lot of these young people stayed on in Poland.
Because Poland tried to hold on.
Look, they were all different.
And sometimes they tried to hold on to the few Jews, and they gave them very good scholarships to study, whoever wanted.
This is why I stayed on and I studied.
But sometimes there were [NON-ENGLISH] and there were very-- things that-- that Jews did run away.
You know it was a very-- the situation was really all the time, all these years you didn't know either you should stay or you should go.
Or you didn't know, either you did the right thing, or the wrong thing.
You know, you did things hoping that this is the right thing.
And so in 1945-1946, we were trying to help the remaining Jews, the ones that were in Poland, with whatever we could.
When the war officially ended, we were in Dzmabul.
What-- well, we didn't know anything.
We only heard that-- that in Russian that our army, that the Red Army took Berlin, and that the war is finished.
And of course, look, it was for us.
It was a very big thing.
This was the day of triumph.
Because we knew that-- we knew one thing.
We didn't know what-- what Hitler did to the Jews.
But we knew we run away from it.
Because we were thinking that this is what he will do.
So when the war finished, it was so-- for us, like a day of liberation.
And we were starting to plan how to go back to Poland.
It was a very-- I mean in Russia the situation was also-- on the one hand the Russians were-- I mean it was a triumph.
On the other hands, they lost a lot of-- you know, there wasn't a family that wasn't affected by the war, even the Russians.
The Jews lost a lot of people.
I think the Russians lost 20 million people.
So you know what-- what the Germans-- what the Nazis did is-- is a terrible thing.
When you think back how many people they annihilated, it's just unbelievable.
Yeah.
So-- so 1945, I was with my parents.
And we tried-- we tried to do so-- resume, we tried to make the best of a bad situation.
Because it was a terrible situation.
My mother was very depressed.
My father was very depressed.
Everybody that you met lost his family.
So there was no-- so like-- like at the beginning you were hoping.
You were thinking, now it was a hopeless situation.
All right.
We've survived.
And that was-- that was wonderful.
But everybody around you disappeared.
It was a-- so it was a very-- it was a very-- so full of tragedies.
You-- every day you would hear another story.
People were talking about concentration.
We became aware that 1945, when we came back to Poland, we were aware what was happening.
I mean and the Poles sort of tried.
The situation was that the Poles were very-- in Katowice I've seen things like with the [NON-ENGLISH].
That the Poles would put them in camps and prisons, which we felt was very justified.
But still for us, it was many Jews tried to get out of Poland, as I said before.
I wanted to study-- you know my dream was to study.
And now I could feel that for the first time in my life I had all the opportunities, because I got a scholarship.
